title: The alternating square knot
subtitle: Staggered rows of square knots that build an open diamond lattice
excerpt: The alternating square knot is the standard open-mesh structure in macramé: square knots tied in staggered rows, each row offset by two cords from the one above. It is what gives wall hangings, plant hangers, and bag bodies their diamond lattice. Once you have the rule, you can build any width.
sourceNotes: The alternating square knot is a standard macramé structure. Observation-led.

content.text: is the open-mesh structure that gives most macramé its diamond lattice, used for wall hangings, plant hangers, and bag bodies. The trick is one rule: tie square knots in groups of four cords on the first row, then shift the grouping across by two cords for the next row, and back again on the row after. The diamonds appear automatically once two rows are in place.
What makes it tricky is keeping the spacing even between rows; uneven gaps give uneven diamonds. Pin each row to the macramé board at a consistent depth before tying.

items.qty: 8 cords, each 120 cm (for a practice swatch)
items.name: Cotton macramé cord, 3 mm single-strand

content.text: Mount eight cords onto the dowel using lark's head knots. You now have 16 working ends.

Tie row 1. Starting from the left, tie a square knot using cords 1, 2, 3, 4 (cords 1 and 4 are the working cords; 2 and 3 are the core). Continue across: knot cords 5 to 8, then 9 to 12, then 13 to 16. Row 1 is four square knots.

Tie row 2 with the offset. Leave cords 1 and 2 hanging for now. Tie a square knot using cords 3, 4, 5, 6. Continue: knot cords 7 to 10, then 11 to 14. Leave cords 15 and 16 hanging. Row 2 is three square knots, offset by two cords. Leave a gap of around 2 cm between rows for a standard open mesh; a tighter gap gives a denser mesh.

Tie row 3 back at the row 1 grouping: cords 1 to 4, 5 to 8, 9 to 12, 13 to 16. The diamonds should now be visible between rows 1 and 2. Carry on alternating groupings for as many rows as the piece needs.

The vertical gap between rows is what controls how open the mesh is. A 3 cm gap gives a loose, airy lattice. A 1 cm gap gives a compact mesh where the diamonds are more closed. Patterns usually specify the gap in centimetres; pin each row at the correct depth before tying if precision matters.

attrs.intro: Two common errors when building alternating square knot panels.

items.fix: Pin the mounting dowel and measure the drop to each row before tying. Consistent spacing produces even diamonds.
items.cause: Row spacing was not kept consistent between first-row and second-row knots.
items.symptom: Diamonds are uneven, some large, some small

items.fix: The two outermost cords (one at each edge) should only appear as working cords in the rows where they belong, never as core cords. Re-read the grouping before each row.
items.cause: Edge cords are being used as working cords in one row and as core cords in another, compressing the edges.
items.symptom: Edges pulling inward over several rows

attrs.heading: Common problems
